splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuit courts disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions for withdrawal liability calculations to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may select assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be determined by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), giving employers greater predictability about potential liability.

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that a plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing the use of more current information in withdrawal liability calculations.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Federal circuits are split on whether courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending court-ordered conditions beyond the time specified in the judgment when a person absconds. This affects sentencing calculations at revocation hearings for thousands of individuals who abscond each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that the term of supervised release is tolled when a supervisee absconds, allowing courts to revoke supervised release even after the original term would have expired.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory authority to toll supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and that judicially-created tolling doctrines may not extend the congressionally specified term.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether federal prisoners face a categorical bar under § 2244(b)(1) from raising any claim they previously presented in a successive § 2255 motion, in addition to the separate requirements of § 2255(h). This affects the gatekeeping requirements for federal prisoners seeking collateral review of their convictions.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that the categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1) on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, imposing a stricter gatekeeping requirement.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that federal prisoners need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements (newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law) and are not subject to the additional categorical bar of § 2244(b)(1).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the standing requirements for candidates seeking to challenge election rules before an election takes place. The split concerns whether candidates suffer a cognizable injury from state election procedures that allegedly dilute lawful votes, with implications for pre-election challenges to voting rules nationwide.

positions.label: Standing exists
positions.summary: Holds that candidates have Article III standing to bring pre-election challenges to state election laws based on competitive injury or vote-dilution theories.

positions.label: Standing does not exist
positions.summary: Holds that candidates lack Article III standing to challenge state election laws before the election because the alleged injury is too speculative or generalized.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes priority over judicial deference to agency interpretations (Auer/Kisor deference) when construing ambiguous sentencing guidelines provisions that expand criminal liability.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether lenity trumps deference to the Sentencing Commission's commentary when a Guidelines provision is ambiguous in a criminal context. The Third Circuit held that agency deference trumps lenity, while at least three other circuits hold that lenity prevails. The issue is particularly important after Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo overruled Chevron deference.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretation.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that deference to the Sentencing Commission's authoritative interpretation of its own Guidelines prevails over the rule of lenity, even in criminal sentencing contexts.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines commentary (Application Note 1) can authoritatively expand the definition of 'controlled substance offense' under U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2(b) to include inchoate offenses like conspiracy, when the guideline text does not mention them.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which adds conspiracy and other inchoate offenses to the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is a valid interpretation entitled to deference under Stinson/Kisor or an impermissible expansion of the guideline text. This affects career-offender designations for many federal defendants.

positions.label: Commentary is authoritative
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is an authoritative and binding interpretation of § 4B1.2(b), and conspiracy qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' for career-offender purposes.

positions.label: Commentary impermissibly expands guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the plain text of § 4B1.2(b) unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that Application Note 1 cannot add to the guideline what the text omits, particularly after Kisor narrowed deference to agency interpretations.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any single criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' means 'and' or 'or' in the safety-valve provision. This determines whether defendants must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief or whether meeting any one criterion is disqualifying, which directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' in its ordinary conjunctive sense.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any single criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria is disqualifying from safety-valve relief, reading 'and' as distributive or disjunctive in context.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether non-retroactive changes in law, such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c), can constitute or contribute to 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether non-retroactive sentencing reforms can be considered as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release. Some circuits allow courts to consider such changes, while others hold that non-retroactive changes in law cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons. The Sentencing Commission's 2023 policy statement amendment (§ 1B1.13(b)(6)) attempted to resolve this but some circuits question the Commission's authority.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor among others when evaluating compassionate release motions, particularly when combined with other circumstances.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state statute covers substances not scheduled under the federal Controlled Substances Act.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the underlying substance to be federally scheduled. Some circuits hold the definition broadly encompasses any state drug offense, while others require a match to federal schedules. This affects base offense levels and career-offender designations.

positions.label: No federal-schedule limitation
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' broadly includes offenses under state law regardless of whether the specific substance is federally controlled, because there is no textual basis to graft a federal law limitation onto the guideline.

positions.label: Must match federal schedules
positions.summary: Holds that a state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance is also controlled under federal law, applying the categorical approach to require correspondence between state and federal drug schedules.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's asset belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether appreciation in property value between the filing of a Chapter 13 petition and conversion to Chapter 7 inures to the bankruptcy estate or remains with the debtor. The Ninth Circuit held that such appreciation belongs to the estate, while other courts have held it belongs to the debtor, creating a circuit split with significant implications for debtor-creditor relations in bankruptcy.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that the plain language of § 348(f)(1)(A), combined with the broad definition of 'property of the estate' in § 541(a), means post-petition appreciation inures to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that the Bankruptcy Code's structure and § 348(f)(1)(A)'s purpose of encouraging good-faith conversion mean post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation belongs to the debtor, not the estate.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the civil or criminal appeal deadline applies to an order denying attorney's fees under the Hyde Amendment in a criminal case.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (subject to the 30-day civil appeal deadline) or part of the criminal case itself (subject to the 14-day criminal appeal deadline). The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the 30-day civil appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(a) applies.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment fee motion is part of the criminal case and the 14-day criminal appeal deadline under Fed. R. App. P. 4(b) applies.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may impose revocation sentences under 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e) to punish a defendant for violations of supervised release, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to facilitating the defendant's reentry into the community.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on the permissible purposes of supervised release revocation sentences. The Sixth Circuit allows courts to expressly punish defendants at revocation, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences must focus on the defendant's reentry and rehabilitation rather than punishment for the violation itself.

positions.label: Punishment is permissible
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose revocation sentences to punish defendants for supervised release violations, not limited to facilitating reentry.

positions.label: Revocation must focus on reentry, not punishment
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release revocation sentences should be guided by the defendant's need for rehabilitation and reentry support, not retribution for the violation.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the denial of a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine as an order involving a value of 'high order' that would be effectively unreviewable after final judgment.
splits.description: Circuits have not uniformly addressed whether denial of a motion to seal medical records qualifies for immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy is a value of sufficiently 'high order' to warrant immediate review, while other circuits have been more restrictive about extending the collateral order doctrine to sealing disputes.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that the denial of a motion to seal medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' effectively unreviewable after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal generally are not immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine, as the party may seek review after final judgment or through mandamus.

circuits.name: Sixth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Shane Grp., Inc. v. Blue Cross Blue Shield of Mich.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the FAAAA's preemption clause, which bars states from enacting or enforcing laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any . . . broker,' preempts common-law negligent hiring and selection claims against freight brokers when the broker's driver causes an accident. This affects the liability landscape for the freight brokerage industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are preempted because they relate to the broker's 'service' of arranging transportation and would effectively regulate broker operations.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt broker negligence claims
positions.summary: Holds that common-law negligence claims against freight brokers are not preempted because they do not regulate prices, routes, or services within the meaning of the FAAAA, and traditional tort duties fall outside the statute's preemptive scope.

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for a sentence reduction under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether compassionate release motions may encompass claims that overlap with habeas corpus relief under § 2255, such as arguments that the underlying conviction or sentence was legally erroneous. Some circuits permit consideration of such factors as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' inquiry, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider legal errors in the underlying conviction or sentence as one factor in the compassionate release analysis, as the statute imposes no categorical bar on such considerations.

positions.label: May not consider — use § 2255 instead
positions.summary: Holds that § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for challenging the legality of the conviction or sentence itself, and such claims must be raised through habeas corpus under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful users of controlled substances, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen, circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by unlawful drug users is constitutional as applied to marijuana users. Some circuits have upheld the statute, while others have found it unconstitutional, creating divergent rules on gun rights for marijuana users across the country.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because historical tradition supports disarming those who use intoxicating substances.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming those who use controlled substances.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether FIFRA's labeling requirements preempt state tort claims alleging that a pesticide manufacturer should have included additional warnings on its label. This affects products liability litigation involving herbicides and pesticides nationwide, including high-profile Roundup litigation.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims because allowing such claims would impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' those required under FIFRA.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state failure-to-warn claims are not preempted by FIFRA because they parallel, rather than impose requirements 'in addition to or different from,' federal labeling requirements.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, exempting them from mandatory arbitration.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to construe the FAA's Section 1 exemption for transportation workers. The question of whether last-mile delivery workers who distribute goods to retail stores are 'transportation workers' engaged in interstate commerce has divided the circuits, with implications for millions of workers in logistics and distribution chains.

positions.label: Broad exemption — last-mile distributors qualify
positions.summary: Holds that workers who distribute goods that have traveled in interstate commerce, including last-mile delivery workers, are 'transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce' exempt from the FAA.

positions.label: Narrow exemption — last-mile distributors do not qualify
positions.summary: Holds that the Section 1 exemption is limited to workers in the transportation industry who are directly engaged in the movement of goods across borders or state lines, not to workers who merely handle goods that previously traveled in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of race discrimination under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 in the operation of hospitality or business establishments, particularly regarding the scope of the right to make and enforce contracts?
splits.description: Circuits have taken different approaches to the standard for § 1981 claims in hospitality and service contexts, particularly regarding what constitutes interference with the right to make and enforce contracts and the available remedies. The Supreme Court granted certiorari to resolve the Second Circuit's approach against other circuits' frameworks.

positions.label: Narrower standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 claims require a showing of interference with an actual contractual relationship or attempted contract, applying a more restrictive view of what constitutes actionable discrimination.

positions.label: Broader standard for § 1981 claims
positions.summary: Holds that § 1981 covers a broader range of discriminatory conduct in business establishments, including degraded service or hostile treatment that impairs the enjoyment of contracted services.
